items.title: Gold Holds Gains As Weak US Jobs Data Lowers Rate Hike Odds
items.summary: Gold held two days of gains after weak US jobs data reduced the chances of the Federal Reserve raising interest rates this year to tackle inflation.

items.title: 'Most massive' Russian attack on Kyiv kills at least 27
items.summary: The latest barrage deployed the largest number of weapons on the capital and hit locations over a wide area.

items.title: As Ukraine War Escalates, Witkoff and Kushner Are Focused on Iran
items.summary: Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are pivotal players at a moment when the posts of U.S. ambassador to Moscow and Kyiv are both vacant.

items.title: With a Fragile Ceasefire under Threat, What Future for the Strait of Hormuz?
items.summary: The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran prompted Tehran to close the Strait of Hormuz, allowing only selected ships to pass through. In this Q&A, Crisis Group experts Yasmine Farouk, Chris Newton and Ali Vaez consider what could come next in this vital maritime trade corridor.

items.title: Japan Cuts Gas in Favor of Coal as Hormuz Disruption Chokes LNG
items.summary: Japan sharply reduced natural gas-fired power generation last month, instead relying more on coal, as disruptions around the Strait of Hormuz tightened supplies of the cleaner-burning fuel.

items.title: Oil Steadies as More Supply Flows Through Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: Oil steadied as tanker flows through the Strait of Hormuz increased further, adding to a gush of near-term supply.

items.title: МВБ: Секретная служба США не предотвратила покушение на Трампа из-за ошибок
items.summary: В отчете подчеркивается, что сотрудники службы в июле 2024 года не зафиксировали то, что Томас Крукс осматривал местность с помощью беспилотника

items.title: Senegal’s World Cup agony: Nation left rueing last-gasp collapse
items.summary: Former players and fans blame substitutions, lack of focus, and travel restrictions for Senegal's World Cup exit.

items.title: Australia expects to gain extra US$26 billion from exports after Iran war raises prices
items.summary:
Australia forecasts a A$38 billion (US$26 billion) lift in its export income as commodity and energy prices rise because of the war in Iran.
The nation’s resources exports are set to rise almost 3 per cent to A$416 billion in the financial year through June 2027, the Department of Industry, Science and Resources said in its latest resources and energy quarterly on Friday. The extra earnings assume

items.title: A Long Gulf War Can Starve the World. A Hormuz Transit Deal Could Save Millions.
EU and NATO Members Grapple with War in Iran
items.summary: This week on War & Peace, Olga speaks with Crisis Group’s head of EU Affairs, Lisa Musiol, about how the EU, its members and European members of NATO have responded to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran and what it reveals about EU foreign policy and its constraints.
